summary:
The Theban Hegemony (371–362 BC) was a brief period of Theban dominance over Greece following the stunning victory at [[event:1020|Battle of Leuctra]], during which Thebes under Epaminondas and Pelopidas became the leading Greek military power. The hegemony demonstrated that Thebes could project power but also exposed the limits of any single polis's ability to impose lasting order on the fractious Greek world.

Epaminondas led multiple campaigns into the Peloponnese, liberating Messenia from Spartan control and founding the city of Megalopolis as a bulwark against Spartan recovery. These acts effectively ended Sparta's ability to field its traditional armies, which depended on helot labor freed by Messenian liberation. Thebes also intervened in Macedonia, taking the young Philip II as a hostage — where he observed Epaminondas's innovative tactics at close range, lessons he would later apply in building his own army. The [[event:1022|Sacred Band of Thebes]] remained undefeated throughout. The hegemony ended at the Battle of Mantinea in 362 BC, where Epaminondas was killed in the moment of victory, depriving Thebes of its indispensable commander and leaving Greece without a clear hegemon.
